section.title: Esther's Banquet and Haman's Gallows
section.sectionRationale: Esther risks the unsummoned approach, and the scepter is extended: she finds favor and lives. But instead of pleading at once, she invites the king and Haman to a banquet, and then to a second banquet the next day. The delay looks strange, yet it sets the timing for everything that follows. Haman leaves the first feast elated, only to see Mordecai unbowed at the gate, and his joy curdles into rage. Egged on by his wife and friends, he builds a towering gallows for Mordecai, the very instrument that will destroy him.
section.thesis: Grace grants Esther access and life, and a hidden providence rules even her delay; meanwhile a proud man, unable to enjoy all he has because of one unbowed Jew, prepares the snare that will catch himself.
section.frameNote: Three movements: Esther approaching and the scepter extended; the first banquet and a puzzling second invitation; and Haman's rage that raises a gallows for Mordecai.

verses.text: On the third day, Esther put on her royal robes and stood in the inner court of the palace across from the king’s quarters. The king was sitting on his royal throne in the royal courtroom, facing the entrance.

As soon as the king saw Queen Esther standing in the court, she found {{chen|favor}} in his sight. The king extended the gold scepter in his hand toward Esther, and she approached and touched the tip of the scepter.
“What is it, Queen Esther?” the king inquired. “What is your request? Even up to half the kingdom, it will be given to you.”
“If it pleases the king,” Esther replied, “may the king and Haman come today to the banquet I have prepared for the king.”
“Hurry,” commanded the king, “and bring Haman, so we can do as Esther has requested.” So the king and Haman went to the banquet that Esther had prepared.

units.label: The banquet and a second invitation

verses.text: And as they drank their wine, the king said to Esther, “What is your petition? It will be given to you. What is your request? Even up to half the kingdom, it will be fulfilled.”
Esther replied, “This is my petition and my request:

If I have found favor in the sight of the king, and if it pleases the king to grant my petition and fulfill my request, may the king and Haman come tomorrow to the banquet I will prepare for them. Then I will answer the king’s question.”

units.label: Haman's rage and the gallows
units.note: the snare he builds for himself

verses.text: That day Haman went out full of joy and glad of heart. At the king’s gate, however, he saw Mordecai, who did not rise or tremble in fear at his presence. And Haman was filled with {{chemah|rage}} toward Mordecai.
Nevertheless, Haman restrained himself and went home. And calling for his friends and his wife Zeresh,
Haman recounted to them his glorious wealth, his many sons, and all the ways the king had honored and promoted him over the other officials and servants.
“What is more,” Haman added, “Queen Esther invited no one but me to join the king at the banquet she prepared, and I am invited back tomorrow along with the king.
Yet none of this satisfies me as long as I see Mordecai the Jew sitting at the king’s gate.”

His wife Zeresh and all his friends told him, “Have them build a {{ets|gallows}} fifty cubits high, and ask the king in the morning to have Mordecai hanged on it. Then go to the banquet with the king and enjoy yourself.” The advice pleased Haman, and he had the gallows constructed.

chen.gloss: Favor, grace, the unearned goodwill of a superior. Esther finding favor is a refrain of the book, first with the harem keeper, then the king, and now at the moment of mortal risk. It is the quiet vocabulary of grace: undeserved acceptance that grants access and life, a small window onto the greater favor God shows His people.

chemah.gloss: Hot anger, wrath, burning rage. Haman is filled with chemah at the sight of one unbowed Jew, and the same word describes the king's fury later in the book. Here it exposes the emptiness of pride: a man with everything is poisoned by a single slight, and his rage drives him to build the very gallows that will hang him.

ets.gloss: A tree, wood, or wooden pole; here the gallows, an impaling stake or beam some seventy five feet high. Haman raises it for Mordecai, but by the book's grim justice he will die on it himself. Scripture's pattern is that the wicked fall into the pit they dig, and the enemy's instrument of death becomes his own undoing.

basic.context: Esther steps into the inner court, and the king, pleased at the sight of her, extends the golden scepter: she is welcomed, and she lives. He offers her anything, up to half the kingdom. But rather than plead on the spot, she invites him and Haman to a banquet, and then, at the banquet, to a second banquet the next day. Haman goes home thrilled at the honor, until he passes Mordecai, who still will not bow. His delight sours into rage. He brags to his wife and friends about all his wealth and status, then admits it means nothing while Mordecai lives. So they urge him to build a huge gallows and ask the king to hang Mordecai on it in the morning. Delighted, Haman builds it.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter raises two questions. First, why does Esther delay? The narrator does not say, but the pause turns out to be perfectly timed, leaving room for the sleepless night and reversal to come. Second, what does Haman teach us? He is a study in pride: he has everything and enjoys none of it because of one man's refusal to bow. The abiding lessons are the providence that governs even a delay and the self-destroying nature of pride, which builds its own gallows.
basic.originalLanguages: Esther finds favor, chen, the book's recurring word for unearned goodwill; grace grants her access and life. Haman is filled with rage, chemah, burning anger that a single slight kindles in a man who has everything. And the advice is to build a gallows, an ets, a wooden pole; unknown to Haman, it is his own death he is raising. The words quietly announce grace, the emptiness of pride, and the coming reversal.
basic.typology: The gallows Haman builds for the righteous Mordecai, and on which he himself will die, is a pattern that reaches its climax at the cross. There the enemy raised an instrument of death for the Righteous One and was himself disarmed and defeated by it. Esther clothed in royalty, welcomed by the scepter of grace, pictures the believer clothed in Christ and granted bold access to the King.
basic.christ: Haman prepares a gallows to destroy the man who would not bow, and ends up destroyed by it. This is the shape of the gospel: the powers of evil raised a cross to destroy the sinless Son of God, and through that very death He disarmed and triumphed over them. The scepter extended to Esther, granting her life and a hearing, pictures the grace by which sinners are welcomed into the presence of the King through Christ.
basic.god: God is not named, and yet He is clearly at work. Esther's favor, her strange delay, the timing that will soon expose Haman, all of it moves toward rescue under a hand the narrator never mentions. Meanwhile Haman shows what a life without God looks like: endlessly craving honor, never satisfied, undone by pride. The chapter teaches that God rules the timing of deliverance and that pride carries the seed of its own ruin.
basic.crossReferences: Proverbs 16 warns that pride goes before destruction. Psalm 7 describes the wicked man falling into the pit he has dug. Proverbs 11 says the righteous is delivered from trouble and the wicked takes his place. And Colossians 2 shows the cross turning the enemy's weapon into his defeat.
basic.oneStory: Grace welcomes Esther and a hidden God rules even her delay, while a proud man, unsatisfied by all his honors, builds the gallows that will hang him. It is a small picture of the great reversal at the heart of the Bible: the instrument meant to destroy the righteous becomes the undoing of the enemy, fulfilled when Christ conquered through the cross.

context.literary: The chapter pairs two scenes: Esther's poised, patient approach to the king, and Haman's swelling, brittle pride. Between them sits the puzzle of the delay, a narrative pause that ratchets up tension and, as the reader will soon see, makes room for the turning point. The gallows rising at the chapter's end hangs over everything that follows, a loaded image awaiting its ironic reversal.
context.historical: Persian banquets were settings for serious business, and a queen's private banquet for the king and his chief minister signaled high favor, which is why Haman is so flattered. A gallows fifty cubits high, around seventy five feet, is likely an impaling stake meant for gruesome public display, an intimidation as much as an execution. Haman's boasting of wealth and many sons reflects the honor culture of the court, where status was everything.
context.canonical: Haman building the gallows that will destroy him joins a biblical pattern: the schemer caught in his own scheme, the pit-digger falling into his own pit. Esther's favor and access echo the Bible's theme of grace granting the unworthy a hearing before the king, and the whole reversal anticipates the pattern of the cross, where the enemy's apparent triumph becomes his defeat.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show grace granting Esther life and a hearing, to expose the restless emptiness of Haman's pride, and to set in place, through Esther's delay and Haman's gallows, the timing and the irony that a hidden God will use to overturn the whole plot.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Esther's shrewd delay and Haman's proud rage; it prescribes neither a formula for petitioning kings nor, obviously, Haman's cruelty. It commends discernment and courage, and it warns against the pride that cannot rest, holding out the deeper truth of God's providential timing.

debates.question: Why does Esther delay with a second banquet instead of asking at the first?

views.label: Providential timing
views.case: The narrator gives no reason, and the delay proves decisive: that night the king cannot sleep, Mordecai is honored, and Haman is humiliated before the second banquet ever begins. On this reading the pause is the fingerprint of the hidden God, arranging events so that the reversal falls into place at exactly the right hour, beyond Esther's calculation.

views.label: Esther's wisdom
views.case: Others emphasize Esther's prudence: she reads the moment, secures the king's curiosity and goodwill, lulls Haman into fatal overconfidence, and chooses the setting where an accusation against the king's favorite is most likely to land. The delay is shrewd stagecraft, the discernment of a wise woman handling a volatile king and a dangerous enemy.

debates.bearing: The two readings are not rivals: Esther's wisdom and God's providence run together, so that her carefully chosen timing turns out to be God's timing, and the reader learns to trust the Lord even in a delay whose purpose is not yet clear.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the chapter. Esther finds favor, chen, the book's steady refrain for unearned goodwill; grace opens the throne room and grants her life. Haman is filled with chemah, burning rage, the emptiness of pride laid bare when a man who has amassed wealth, sons, and honors is undone by one unbowed Jew. And his household urges him to raise an ets, a wooden gallows some seventy five feet high, meant for Mordecai; the very word foreshadows the reversal, for Haman will hang on it himself. Grace, pride, and irony are all seeded in the vocabulary before the plot pays them out.

typology.type: The gallows Haman builds for the righteous, on which he himself dies
typology.fulfillment: The cross, where the enemy's instrument of death against the Righteous One became the means of his own defeat, Colossians 2:14-15 and Hebrews 2:14.

typology.type: Esther clothed in royalty, welcomed by the extended scepter
typology.fulfillment: Believers clothed in Christ's righteousness and granted bold access to the throne of grace, Galatians 3:27 and Ephesians 3:12.
The self-destroying nature of sin and pride, which God resists and brings low, Proverbs 16:18 and James 4:6.

study.christ: The gallows is the heart of the chapter's gospel shape. Haman raises a towering instrument of death to destroy the man who would not bow, and by the book's end he swings on it himself. This is precisely what happened at Calvary: the powers of evil raised a cross to destroy the sinless Son of God, and in that very death Christ disarmed the rulers and authorities and triumphed over them, Colossians 2:15, destroying through death the one who holds the power of death, Hebrews 2:14. Esther, meanwhile, pictures the access that Christ secures: she is welcomed by the extended scepter and given a hearing, as believers, clothed in Christ, now approach the throne with boldness, Ephesians 3:12. The wicked man's snare and the queen's grace together point to the King who saves by reversal.
study.god: Once again God is unnamed, and once again He governs everything. Esther's favor with the king, the strange delay that leaves room for the coming night, the timing that will expose Haman at the perfect moment, all of it moves under a providence the narrator never labels. Set against this quiet sovereignty is Haman, a portrait of life curved in on itself: he has wealth, sons, and the highest honors, and enjoys none of them because one man will not bow. God resists the proud, and here we watch pride begin to build its own scaffold. The Lord rules the timing of rescue and lets the arrogant be snared by their own schemes.

crossReferences.note: Pride goes before destruction; Haman's swelling arrogance is already raising the gallows that will bring him down.
The wicked man digs a pit and falls into it; his violence returns on his own head, the pattern of Haman's gallows.
The righteous is delivered from trouble, and the wicked takes his place, fulfilled when Mordecai is spared and Haman hanged.
At the cross the enemy's instrument of death became his defeat, the great reversal foreshadowed by Haman's gallows.

study.oneStory: Grace welcomes Esther through the extended scepter, and a hidden God rules even her puzzling delay, while a proud man, unsatisfied by all his wealth and honor, raises the gallows that will hang him. It is a miniature of the Bible's great reversal: the instrument meant to destroy the righteous becomes the undoing of the enemy. What is played out in irony here is fulfilled in earnest at the cross, where Christ conquered death by dying.

sermon.title: O Father, You Are Sovereign!
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in Esther, this message expounds Esther 5, Esther's courageous approach and delay set against Haman's pride and gallows, tracing the sovereignty of the unseen God over the whole scene. A faithful, expository handling of this movement.

quiz.q: What happened when Esther approached the king unsummoned?

options.text: She found favor, and the king extended the golden scepter, sparing her life.
options.note: Esther 5:2 shows the scepter extended, granting her welcome and safety.

options.text: The king had her arrested for breaking the law.
options.note: The law allowed death, but the king showed favor rather than punishment.

options.text: The king ignored her until she was summoned properly.
options.note: He welcomed her at once and offered up to half the kingdom.

options.text: She lost her nerve and fled the court.
options.note: She stood firm, was received, and made her invitation.

quiz.q: Instead of pleading immediately, what did Esther do?

options.text: She invited the king and Haman to a banquet, and then to a second banquet the next day.
options.note: Esther 5:4-8 records the delay that proves providentially timed.

options.text: She accused Haman openly in the throne room at once.
options.note: She waited for the banquets rather than accusing Haman immediately.

options.text: She asked the king for half the kingdom straight away.
options.note: The king offered that, but Esther's request was for the banquets.

options.text: She said nothing and returned to her quarters.
options.note: She acted, inviting the king and Haman to dine with her.

quiz.q: Why did Haman's joy turn to rage?

options.text: He saw Mordecai at the gate, who would not rise or tremble before him.
options.note: Esther 5:9 shows one unbowed Jew souring all of Haman's delight.

options.text: The king had insulted him at the banquet.
options.note: The banquet had honored Haman; his rage came from Mordecai's refusal.

options.text: Esther had refused to let him attend.
options.note: Esther had specially invited him; his fury was at Mordecai alone.

options.text: He had lost his wealth and position.
options.note: He still boasted of great wealth and honor; the issue was pride, not loss.

quiz.q: What does Haman's boasting and rage reveal about him?

options.text: The emptiness of pride: he had everything yet could enjoy none of it while one man would not bow.
options.note: Esther 5:11-13 exposes pride that no honor can satisfy.

options.text: That he was a humble man pushed too far.
options.note: The text shows arrogance, not humility; he paraded his status.

options.text: That he had genuine reason to fear for his life.
options.note: His problem was wounded pride, not real danger, at this point.

options.text: That he cared nothing for honor or status.
options.note: He cared intensely for honor, which is why one slight enraged him.

quiz.q: What did Haman build, and why is it significant?

options.text: A towering gallows for Mordecai, on which Haman himself would later be hanged.
options.note: Esther 5:14 sets up the reversal: the snare he built caught himself.

options.text: A monument to celebrate his own greatness.
options.note: He built a gallows for Mordecai, not a monument, and it became his own.

options.text: A prison to hold Mordecai indefinitely.
options.note: It was a gallows for execution, not a prison.

options.text: A gift to present to the king at the banquet.
options.note: It was an instrument of death for Mordecai, prepared in rage.

questions: Esther was welcomed by the extended scepter when she had every reason to fear rejection. How does it change your prayers to know you are welcomed into the King's presence through Christ?
Esther waited when she could have rushed, and her timing proved to be God's timing. Where is God asking you to be patient in a situation whose purpose you cannot yet see?
Haman had wealth, sons, and honor, yet one unbowed man robbed him of all joy. What small slight or ungranted thing is stealing your contentment right now?
Haman's pride drove him to build a gallows that would destroy him. Where might pride be quietly building something in your life that will one day fall on you?
The instrument Haman raised to kill became the means of his own death, a shadow of the cross. How does the reversal at Calvary give you hope when evil seems to be winning?

translationNotes.note: Put on her royal robes translates a phrase that literally reads she clothed herself in royalty, marking the dignity and risk of her approach to the throne.
Found favor, Hebrew chen, is a keyword of the book; the same grace that won Esther the throne now wins her a hearing at the moment of greatest danger.
The gallows fifty cubits high, roughly seventy five feet, is likely an impaling stake for public display; the height underscores both Haman's malice and the irony of his coming fall.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Esther 5:1-14. No dashes were present in the source; none remain. Sermon verified this session from truthforlife.org.
